title: Binary Search Trees
description: Finally revealing what set/map use internally: the BST ordering property, O(log n) search and insert, and why an unbalanced tree degrades to O(n).
objectives: Understand the binary search tree ordering property
Implement search and insert on a BST
Understand why BST operations are O(log n) when balanced
Understand why an unbalanced BST degrades toward O(n)

sections.text: 1. Recap: set/map's O(log n) — Finally Revealed
set and map were both described as "typically implemented as a balanced binary search tree," without explaining what that structure actually looks like. Here it is.
2. What is a Binary Search Tree?
A binary search tree is a binary tree where, for every node, all values in its left subtree are smaller, and all values in its right subtree are larger.

This holds at every node, not just the root — 3's left subtree (1) is smaller than 3, and 3's right subtree (6) is bigger than 3, recursively, all the way down. This ordering is what makes binary search (recall Binary Search) possible on a tree shape instead of a flat array.
This is Binary Search's exact halving logic — "too small, discard the right half; too big, discard the left half" — reimagined on a tree instead of an array index range.

sections.title: Tracing the inserts
points: insert(8): root is empty → 8 becomes root
insert(3): 3 < 8 → go left → empty → 3 becomes root's left child
insert(10): 10 is NOT < 8 → go right → empty → 10 becomes root's right child
insert(6): 6 < 8 → go left to 3. 6 is NOT < 3 → go right → empty → 6 becomes 3's right child
Final shape: 8 with left=3 (right child 6) and right=10

sections.text: 6. Why O(log n) — And Why It Can Degrade

Recall Binary Trees' "balanced" definition. If a BST stays balanced, search and insert are O(log n), exactly like Binary Search's halving argument and Heap's height reasoning. But nothing in the basic insert() logic above actively keeps the tree balanced — inserting values in already-sorted order (1, 2, 3, 4, 5...) produces a tree that's really just a straight line, degrading every operation to O(n), no better than a linked list. Self-balancing variants (which set/map actually use internally) solve this, but are beyond our current scope.

sections.title: A guessing game where every guess halves the remaining range
sections.text: Recall Binary Search's dictionary analogy. A BST is that same halving idea, but as a physical structure: at 8, guessing a number smaller sends you left toward smaller values, guessing bigger sends you right — repeating that at every node you visit, exactly like repeatedly guessing in a number-guessing game that always tells you 'too high' or 'too low.'

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Assuming every BST automatically gives O(log n) — only a balanced BST does; an unbalanced one degrades toward O(n).
Forgetting insert() must reassign node->left / node->right with the recursive call's result — node->left = insert(node->left, value); is what actually attaches the new node.
Getting the comparison direction backward — smaller values always go left, larger (or equal, by convention) values always go right.
Confusing a BST with a heap — a heap only guarantees parent vs children; a BST guarantees an ordering across the ENTIRE left and right subtrees, which is what enables searching by value.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the binary search tree property?
questions.answer: For every node, all values in its left subtree are smaller than the node, and all values in its right subtree are larger.

questions.question: How does searching a BST work?
questions.answer: Compare the target to the current node; if smaller, recurse left; if larger, recurse right; if equal, it's found — stopping at nullptr means not found.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of BST search and insert, and when can it degrade?
questions.answer: O(log n) when the tree is balanced, but it can degrade toward O(n) if the tree becomes unbalanced — for example, inserting already-sorted values produces a tree shaped like a straight line.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A binary search tree maintains an ordering property: for every node, its entire left subtree holds smaller values and its entire right subtree holds larger values. This makes both search and insert follow the exact halving logic of Binary Search — compare against the current node and recurse left or right accordingly — giving O(log n) performance when the tree stays balanced. However, nothing in a basic BST automatically keeps it balanced; inserting values in sorted order produces a tree that's effectively a straight line, degrading every operation to O(n). This is exactly the structure — specifically a self-balancing variant — that set and map use internally.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Binary Search Trees
sections.text: BST = a number-guessing game as a tree. Smaller goes left, bigger goes right, at every node. Balanced = O(log n). Sorted-order inserts = a straight line = O(n).
LeetCode: Binary Search Trees in Practice

sections.title: Search in a Binary Search Tree
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a BST and an integer val, return the subtree rooted at the node with that value, or null if not found.
hints: This is exactly this lesson's own search() function, just returning the node itself instead of a boolean.
Compare val against the current node: equal means found, smaller means go left, larger means go right — never both, unlike a general binary tree search.
Because only one direction is ever explored, this reaches O(log n) on a balanced tree instead of a general tree search's O(n).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Search as if the tree had no ordering property — checking node, then blindly searching BOTH subtrees.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Exploit the BST property: only one subtree can possibly contain val, so only recurse into that one.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log n) on a balanced BST

sections.title: Insert into a Binary Search Tree
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a BST and a value to insert, insert it while keeping the tree a valid BST, and return the root. Any valid resulting tree is acceptable.
hints: This is exactly this lesson's own insert() function — no variation needed at all.
Walk down comparing val to each node, going left or right exactly like search — but instead of stopping at a match, keep going until an EMPTY spot (nullptr) is found.
The new value always becomes a new LEAF — insertion into a BST never needs to rearrange any existing nodes.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Collect all existing values plus the new one, then rebuild a balanced BST from scratch by inserting the sorted middle first.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: This lesson's own insert(): walk down to the correct empty spot and attach a new leaf there — no rebuilding.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log n) on a balanced BST

sections.title: Validate Binary Search Tree
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree, return true if it is a valid binary search tree.

examples.explanation: 6 is in the root's right subtree, so it must be greater than 10 — but 6 < 10, even though 6 is fine relative to its immediate parent 15.

hints: Just checking 'left child < node < right child' at every node is NOT enough — a node deep in the left subtree must be smaller than every ancestor above it, not just its immediate parent.
Pass down a valid (min, max) RANGE as recursion descends: going left tightens the max to the current node's value; going right tightens the min.
A node is valid only if it falls strictly within its inherited (min, max) range — this correctly enforces the constraint from ALL ancestors, not just the direct parent.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Only compare each node against its immediate parent — WRONG, but a common first (buggy) attempt worth recognizing.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Pass a valid (min, max) range down through the recursion, tightened at every step — correctly enforces every ancestor's constraint.

sections.title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree
sections.problemStatement: Given a BST and two nodes p and q, return their lowest common ancestor (the deepest node that has both p and q as descendants, a node can be its own descendant).
hints: In a general binary tree, finding the LCA needs searching both subtrees — but the BST ordering property gives a huge shortcut here.
If both p and q are smaller than the current node, their ancestor must be further LEFT. If both are larger, it must be further RIGHT.
The moment p and q are on DIFFERENT sides of the current node (or one equals the current node), that current node IS the split point — the lowest common ancestor.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Treat it as a general binary tree: recursively search both subtrees for p and q, without exploiting BST ordering.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Exploit BST ordering: follow the single direction where p and q must diverge, no need to explore both subtrees.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log n) on a balanced BST

sections.title: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a BST and an integer k, return the kth smallest value in the tree.
hints: Recall Inorder Traversal's proof: inorder traversal of a BST visits every value in fully sorted order — this fact, not any BST-specific search shortcut, is what solves this problem.
The kth value inorder-visits IS the kth smallest, directly.
An iterative inorder walk with an early-exit counter avoids ever building the full sorted list.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Collect the entire inorder traversal into a vector, then index directly.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Iterative inorder walk with an early exit at the kth visit — never processes more of the tree than necessary.
